speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two. This is the interview with Dr. Eric Nash, as done by Scott Fried on March 26th, 1995, in Forest Hills, New York, United States of America, conducted in English. OK--

speaker: interviewee
text: When--

speaker: interviewer
text: Continue, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: --The Munich Treaty, treason was put into effect, the-- our army was in a state of complete mobilization. We [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] saw what an efficient army we actually had. We were just very small, compared with this giant of Germany. And I personally was moved to the frontier, to the southern frontier, to near [NON-ENGLISH] in Austria. And we were in complete battle readiness, ready to do the fight. And I must say, the spirit was unbelievable. And we were really raring to go. In the army, there were a lot of Germans. Because the Germans were a percentage-wise, a large percentage of the population. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a terrible let down when the whole thing was called off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when that-- when you found out?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were on the front, on the Austrian front line, in full battle gear. I mean, our hospital was set up. And, and we were all ready to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how exactly did you find out that the land had been given away to the, the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was a radio and there were newspapers, and there was loudspeakers and there were announcements and there were assemblies. And we were being kept informed. And it was just terrible. People were crying like children when they heard about this unbelievable. We were so convinced that we expected the English to bomb 'em and the French to start attacking. We had the promise that they would come to help. Instead, they signed this treaty, where they actually threatened to go after us, if we do not comply with conditions of the Munich arrangement.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we, as you know, had a kind of a Maginot Line in the Sudetenland. And the-- all this was given to Hitler on a platter. And it took only a few days, and the trucks came rolling in with the tanks and with the German army. And you-- it's very hard to describe what a shock it was, to see your country invaded by this unbelievable, efficient military machinery. Were you in the Sudetenland when the Germans occupied?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was in southern Moravia. But it is-- it's not the Sudetenland. But it's also a German part of Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what was it like when the Germans eventually, months later, took over the rest of the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they didn't take it right away. They just occupied the Sudeten. And they left a completely defenseless little piece of Bohemian Moravia for a government that you can compare with the Vichy government of France. Our President Benes abdicated and went to London. And there was a Minister Hacha. His name was Hacha, and he was a puppet of the Germans. And he took over government for a few months. And later on, Hitler decided just to occupy the rest of it. And we became what was later named the Bohemian and Moravian Protectorate. And we received a German governor. And the night descended upon us. In this moment, the whole world changed from a most pleasant, peaceful place to a horror place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now at the time, it was just you and your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My mother was still in Vienna at that time. But as soon as-- no, I have to correct that. My mother went-- when Austria was occupied, we worked on it, she should come to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was able to get out?

speaker: interviewee
text: And--oh, yes, she was able to, because I was in the army and I could get all the papers to get her in. And they all moved to Brno, which is the capital of Moravia. And we lived there until to our-- our deportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what were the first signs of, I guess, German anti-Semitism when they occupied?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no sooner were we occupied, the Jewish population was immediately sequestered from the rest of the population. Every day, a new law regulation reigned upon us. And we were stripped of anything that would make life worthwhile.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of these laws?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of these laws?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it started with losing our apartment. And we were moved into an area, which was later like a ghetto. And although we were lived very comfortably in our apartment, we now were all five of us. That means my mother, my sister with her husband, and I and my wife. We were moved into one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you were married at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time I was married. I forgot to mention that when I was demobilized after the Sudeten were given away, I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Erna Knoll, K-N-O-L-L. And we got married-- I was still in uniform-- on November 19th, 1938. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Interestingly enough, we got married a week after the famous or notorious Kristallnacht, where the horror started to become real. And one of my wife's brothers were killed-- was killed in Vienna during the Kristallnacht. Needless to say, that cast a tremendous shadow on our honeymoon, if we can call it this way. But the whole-- our world crumbled and was in such turmoil that we just had to be busy to stay alive and stick around.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after we lost our apartment, the regulation came. We were not allowed to have radio. We had to deliver it. We were not allowed to have any kind of jewelery or furs. We had to deliver it.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a strict curfew. We were, of course, not allowed to be on the street after 6. Needless to say, we were not allowed to use a park. We couldn't go into a movie or, or into a theater.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is all just for the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just for the Jews. We-- our ration cards-- there were already ration cards-- were half of what the other population had. Whenever we were on the street and we happened to meet a German in any kind of a uniform, we had to jump off the sidewalk. We had to get our hats off and stand in attention until he passed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- then came the real humiliation, that every Jew had to wear a Jewish star, the yellow star with the inscription, Jude, which means Jew. And therefore, on the street, we immediately were marked as fair game. Because any hoodlum or any German could do with us whatever they wanted. I myself had a very bad experience. I went with my wife to go to a store. And there was a group of Hitlerjugend rotten kids coming. And they fell on me, and beat me to a pulp. I was unconscious. And later on, I had a broken nose and a broken maxilla. And interestingly enough, we all, of course, were very anxious to emigrate. And we were waiting to get our American visa. We had quite a bit of relations in America, and they all were anxious to help us. But as you know, the American embassy was not a help. It was only an obstacle. And the way I looked was quite a horror. I was black and blue, I had my broken nose. So we went to the general consulate of the United States--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this before the invasion of Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, long before the invasion.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is all before--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was all in 1930-- early of 1939. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I told them, next time, I will not get away with my life. Could I please get my quota, which we were waiting for. It was absolutely deaf ears. They were-- wanted to have no part of it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So our life became worse by the day. And shock after shock hit us. But human nature is very resilient. And we had our unexplained optimism.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were convinced that this is only a temporary affair. And we have to just buckle under, and we will come out all right. Because we listened with anxiousness for the good news, either from, from Germany. Although there were no good news at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reaction of the-- your, I guess, fellow Czech citizens, who weren't Jewish, when all this was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were mixed. Most of our friends stuck to us and helped us. They helped us with food, and they helped us with moral support. They gave us all the news, because we weren't allowed to have a radio. And in, in general, they behaved very well. Of course, there was a Czech fringe, which were delighted that the Jews got it over the head, and that they finally got what they deserved, and so on and so forth. But in-- I must say, the Czech population behaved, in general, fairly well. That changed later on in the war. Because they were so frightened, and they were so helpless that they didn't do too much for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now, how did life change after September, with the invasion of Poland? Did that have a direct effect on Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, life became worse and worse. Our restrictions became worse and worse. And you may question, well, why did we follow all these laws and orders? Well, that was very simple. There was only one punishment if you did not. You were either shot on the spot or we were put into-- into the transports, which was like a horrible threat hanging over you, all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know of transports at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the transports started, actually, much later in the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In 1941, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the first transport left Brno. And unfortunately, my brother was one of the first victims. My brother was a lawyer and specialized in helping people to emigrate.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he-- how was he able to do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he had connections in his profession. And he was able, through his connections, to get visa in very strange countries, whether it was Bulgaria or Ecuador or Peru or Cuba. And he got quite a bit of people out. He himself had two visas in his pocket and was ready to leave. But he was held back. Because he had wanted to help our mother to get out first.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why hadn't he been able to help you and your wife and your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- I-- there were three places, which were more or less accessible for Jews at that time. In the whole wild-- wide world, there were three places. One was Shanghai, which-- where the Chinese established a kind of a Jewish settlement. And if you could get there, which of course was no easy task, you could stay there. Lots of my friends stayed in and survived in Shanghai. The second place was Santo Domingo, who-- there was a little town called Sosua.

speaker: interviewer
text: Interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a few hundred Jews managed to get to Sosua. And Trujillo, at that time, the guy in charge in Santo Domingo--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was looking for a middle class.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Wanted to get the Jews in, because they were good citizens. And the third one was to go illegally to Palestine. That means to have a knapsack over your shoulder and walk through Slovakia and through Romania and through Turkey--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a Zionist?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not a Zionist. But I was willing to go to Palestine. And I was held back by my mother, because she was in no condition-- she was an elderly lady-- to go through all this. My wife and I, we would have been willing to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in Brno then, up until--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was in Brno.

speaker: interviewee
text: And another thing that held me back was that by some strange coincidence, I was chosen to be in charge of the medical service of a refugee camp. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: As a Jew, you were chosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a Jewish refugee camp. And they, they needed a Jewish doctor. Needless to say that after Hitler occupied us, no Jewish doctor was allowed to practice. But I was the exception, because I was hired by the Jewish community to take care of the medical needs of this refugee camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you must have had a very high standing in the community.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The interesting part on this is that when Austria was occupied, a lot of Austrian Jews fled to Czechoslovakia, because we were still unoccupied and free. And they thought they saved their lives by going to Czechoslovakia. Nobody knew what is going-- what the future holds. And a few hundred of these refugees were settled in a old, demobilized factory in a tannery, near Brno, in a little place called Eibenschutz, Ivancice. And I was told by the community and also by the SS people, by the Gestapo-- who took over this refugee camp, and from then on it became not a place of help, but a kind of a concentration camp-- they installed me there, as a chief physician. And I must say, it was a very challenging and highly interesting job. And I really liked what I was doing. That brings us to the way how we could endure all this reign of regulation and dehumanization. It was this constant optimism and the constant hope that this is only passing, and we will be all right. Now that I had a job, we somehow settled with a new situation. And in all the future story of the life, this was the-- the leading motif. You got hit over the head with a new terrible event. And it took you a while to overcome the shock. But then you adjusted to the new situation, although everyone became worse as the time went by.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we were in Eibenschutz, I had a job. I was newly married. We were very happy. And life became halfway tolerable.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were now in a little town. The Czech population was completely on our side and helped us in every way they could. So it was a bearable situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get special privileges or special rations in that you had a job there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't need special ration. Because there was plenty of food. The Czechs supplied us with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Illegally?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- of course we had to wear the yellow star. But I was the only Jew who drove a car. Because I had to go twice a week to Brno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it illegal for others to drive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no other car, no other Jew who could drive a car. And I was stopped all the time. Because there were always road checks. And they were very much surprised that I had a valid license to drive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you have-- were you living in basic fear when you drove and--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. Because at that time, I was awfully young. And as I said, we always lived only for the future. As the years go-- went by, to the refugee camp, came a group of coal miners. There was a coal mine in-- near Ivancice. And I was also in charge of the coal miners. And when, in 1941, when the transports started, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was told that I have nothing to fear, because my services are essential. And I will not be transported out. But these transport became a real horror.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where they were going, or what was to be done?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody knew where they were going. Later on, there was a rumor that we went-- that the transport stopped at a town called Theresienstadt, in the northern Bohemia. And we, of course, didn't know what Theresienstadt meant at that time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Wannsee Conference, as you know, took place in January of 1942. And from this moment on, the machinery got into motion. And then transport after transport was organized and left Brno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any knowledge of, of the war between '39 and '42, as you say, with the Wannsee Conference? Did you know what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. We were fully informed. We-- our Czech friends supplied us with all the news. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: None of it was good.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Some of us had clandestine radios, and we could receive London broadcast. And so we knew what was going on. Unfortunately, the news were no good in the beginning. That was the time of all his stunning, unbelievable victories. And he was just dominating all of Europe and started to plan his invasion of Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when were you transported from Brno?

speaker: interviewee
text: On April 1st of 1942. Let me first tell you about my brother. My brother, being the expert in emigration, had a lot of business to attend to at the Jewish community. And in one of his trips there, he, on the way out, didn't see a high SS officer who came there, to inspect the situation. And he bumped into him. Now, that was a crime at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a simple bump.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just to, to touch a German. And he was immediately put into a transport. We tried all possible interventions. But it was in vain. He left in October of 1941. We-- I now know that his transport went to Minsk, where he stayed alive. He was the transport leader. And he became a high functionary in the Jewish community there. But as you know, the Jewish in the east were liquidated systematically. And he managed to befriend a German soldier, not an SS man. And he--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how do you know all this, by the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I found, when I-- after liberation, we had some gentile friends in Vienna. And they gave me a bundle of letters they received through these soldiers, from my brother. And in this letter, he described what was going on with him. And his last letter was dated in August of 1943. And if you want to, I would like to read--

speaker: interviewer
text: Please--

speaker: interviewee
text: --to you--

speaker: interviewer
text: --by all means.

speaker: interviewee
text: --What this letter said. While he was in Minsk, his wife suffered from a perforated ulcer. And they tried to operate on her on a kitchen table, and she died. And he was really desolate about this loss. And he managed to stay alive 'til '43. In his last letter, it says, 'This will be my last message for a long time, perhaps forever. I have been quite happy in these last few days, since hearing that Irma--' which is our mother-- 'is still alive, but now I must go the way of my fate. I may-- it may well be that the way will reunite me with my beloved darling,' which means his wife, who died. 'If you can, please tell my family that I would dearly have loved to see them again. But frankly, I do not know how.' This was the last communication. And I now know that he was one victim of a mass execution. That was, at that time, the method of extermination, when they had to dig their own graves and strip and were mowed down with machine guns.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so it's-- is it around this time that you were deported or at least moved?

speaker: interviewee
text: Around-- no, I was deported on April 1st, '42. That means before that. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where to?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- at that time, my wife was highly pregnant and expected our baby, which was due on-- in this week that we were transported. So we received our pink slips, which was like a invitation to the transport. And my mother, my sister with her husband, my wife and I, we were bundled up. And we had to go into this school, where all people were collected to, to-- who had to be transported out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what was the feeling there? Was there a real fear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was terrible. Because we had to leave our-- even the last place, although it was not what we used to be, but it was still our home. And we were just uprooted completely. And nobody knew what the future will bring. And we had to undergo our first taste of what life in-- without freedom will be, if you can call it freedom. We were collected in the school. All the windows were darkened. Because the neighbors shouldn't see what was going on there. The sanitary conditions were terrible. There was hardly any water, hardly any food. And we-- it took them three days to strip us whatever valuables we still had and to process us. And we were allowed to have just a little suitcase. And finally, in the night, we were marched to the train station. This march where, mind you, there were old people and there were little children. And-- and there were beatings and there were shootings and there was-- it was quite a horrible let down and a horrible experience. And we finally were put into the train. And it was a long, endless train ride, when we--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the car like, the train itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that-- we-- that was still a normal train. Only in a compartment where there was supposed to be six people, there were 12 people. It was very crowded and uncomfortable. And we were in this train for three days. And then we arrived in Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was just you and your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Theresienstadt was-- that was on the 4th of April, 1942. Theresienstadt was a fortress that Maria Theresa, the empress, Maria Theresa built, with the idea to become a fortress for the weapons that were known in the 18th century. There were huge, massive walls all around the town. There were water-filled moats on the outside. And there were just three or four gates where you could enter the town. Of course, all this was wide open before we got in there. Then it was closed up and became one large jail or, if you want to call, a concentration camp.
